title: Stunting vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Stunting](#dod:stunting) measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Stunting vs. GDP per capita, 2024

Stunting measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of children under five who are stunted and GDP per capita across countries in 2024. The vertical axis displays the stunting rate from 0% to over 50%, while the horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale ranging from $2,000 to $100,000. Points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data shows a negative relationship where higher GDP per capita coincides with lower stunting rates. Countries in the highest stunting band, exceeding 40%, include Burundi, Niger, the Democratic Republic of Congo, East Timor, Angola, and Guatemala, all situated on the left side of the chart with GDP per capita below $10,000. In the middle range, nations like Pakistan, India, and Cambodia show stunting rates between 20% and 35% with GDP per capita roughly between $5,000 and $10,000. Conversely, countries with GDP per capita above $20,000, such as the United States, European nations, and Australia, cluster near the bottom of the chart with stunting rates below 10%. Specific examples in the lower-right quadrant include Brunei, Estonia, and Belarus, which combine high income with stunting rates under 5%. The distribution forms a curve that flattens as income rises, showing that the lowest stunting rates occur exclusively in the highest income bands.
